title: practical english usage - Part 150
content:
grammar *193   adverbs of manner and adjectives For spelling rules, ►    345.

For the adjective well, ►    622.   For the position of adverbs of manner, ►    201.1.

For adjectives ending in -ly, ►     194.1.   For adverbs and adjectives with the same form, ►     194.2.

He was madly in love with her. (     .. .mad in love...) adverb + prepositional phrase not They're playing unusually fast. (     ... unusual fast.) adverb + adverb not This steak is very badly cooked. (     . . .bad cooked.) adverb + past participle not It's terribly cold today. (     .  . .terrible cold.) adverb + adjective prepositional phrases.

These adverbs can also modify adjectives, past participles, other adverbs and 2 other     uses:   terribly        cold For the use of adjectives after linking verbs like look or seem, >11.

She talks funny.

informal style, especially in American English (►   194.4).

But note that some adjective forms are sometimes used as adverbs in an not I don't remember him  very well. (     .. .very good.) not She sang badly. (     She sang bad.) not She danced happily into the room. (     She danced happy...) verb + adverb adjectives (happy, terrible, etc). Adjectives are not used to modify verbs.

Adverbs of manner can modify verbs. They  should not be confused with Examples: happily, terribly, fast, badly, well Adverbs of manner typically say how  something happens or is done.

1  adverbs      of  manner      with    verbs:    He  sang      badly.

193 adverbs      of  manner      and   adjectives grammar •  194   adverbs or adjectives: confusing cases For the adverb of degree fairly, ►    460.

to play fair  to fight fair  to hit/win something fair and square fair Fair is used as an adverb after a verb in some expressions.

Easy come, easy go.     Easier said than done.

Go easy! (= Not too fast!)   Take it easy! (= Relax!) easy Easy is used as an adverb in some informal expressions.

50%  cheaper - order direct from the factory!

The plane goes direct from London to Houston without stopping.

direct Direct is often used informally as an adverb.

Cyanide is a deadly poison. She was fatally injured in the crash.

this meaning is fatally. Compare: Note that deadly is an adjective, meaning 'fatal', 'causing death'. The  adverb for right, dead slow, dead straight, dead sure, dead tired.

'completely' or 'very'. Examples: dead ahead, dead certain, dead drunk, dead dead The  adverb dead is used in certain expressions to mean 'exactly', The explosion blew the cooker clean through the wall.

Sorry I didn't turn up -1 clean forgot.

expressions of movement.

clean The  adverb clean means 'completely' before forget (informal) and some detailed information, check  in a good dictionary.

There is usually a difference of meaning or use. Some examples follow; for more two forms (e.g. late and lately), one like the adjective and the other with -ly.

fast, if you do hard work, you work hard. In other cases, the adverb may have Some adjectives and adverbs have the same form: for example, a fast car goes two    forms 2 adjectives        and   adverbs      with    the   same    form;    adverbs      with It's a daily paper. It comes out daily. an early train I got up early.

and adverbs.

Daily, weekly, monthly, yearly, early and leisurely are both adjectives not He gave a silly laugh, (    He hHtghetLsilfy:) not She smiled in a friendly way. (    She^miled friendly,) There are no adverbs friendly/friendlily, lovely/lovelily, etc.

She gave me a friendly smile.  Her singing was lovely.

ugly, unlikely.

examples: costly, cowardly, deadly, friendly, likely, lively, lonely, lovely, silly, Some words ending in -ly are adjectives, and not normally adverbs. Common 1  adjectives        ending      in  -ly:  friendly,        lively 194 adverbs      or  adjectives:      j:onf   using    cases Section Adverbs     and   Adverbials              16 grammar •  194   adverbs or adjectives: confusing cases My friends are mostly non-smokers.

Mostly means ‘ mainly’ , ‘ most often’  or 'in most cases’ .

You're a most unusual person.
